title: Dog Vaginal Discharge: Why Thick Pus is a Veterinary Emergency
meta_desc: Thick pus from a dog's vagina is a sign of a life-threatening infection called pyometra. Learn why immediate emergency vet care is required.
content_html:
TL;DR: Thick, foul-smelling vulvar discharge in unspayed dogs is a sign of pyometra, a life-threatening uterine infection that requires immediate emergency veterinary intervention.

What causes thick or foul-smelling discharge in my female dog?

  • Thick, foul-smelling, or creamy discharge from a female dog's vulva is most commonly a sign of pyometra, a severe bacterial infection of the uterus.
  • This condition occurs most frequently in unspayed dogs, typically a few weeks after their last heat cycle.
  • The infection causes the uterus to fill with pus, which leaks out visibly if the cervix is open.

Is foul-smelling discharge from my dog's vulva a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a life-threatening medical emergency.
  • Pyometra can quickly lead to sepsis, organ failure, or a ruptured uterus, which is often fatal without immediate intervention.
  • Even if your dog seems otherwise normal, the presence of pus indicates a high level of toxicity in her body.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How does a photo of the discharge help the veterinarian triage my pet?

  • Providing your veterinarian with a clear photograph of the discharge and the surrounding area can be vital for a quick assessment.
  • A photo allows the medical team to evaluate the color, consistency, and volume of the discharge.
  • This visual information helps differentiate between less severe issues like vaginitis and a critical pyometra before you even reach the hospital.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, a thick, purulent (pus-containing) vaginal discharge is a key indicator of pyometra, a potentially life-threatening uterine infection. When the cervix is open, this discharge, which may also contain blood, is readily apparent. Other signs of pyometra include lethargy, anorexia, polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, dehydration, and uterine enlargement. Although fever is not always present, the condition can rapidly progress to shock and death, necessitating prompt veterinary intervention. While a leukogram may be normal, leukocytosis with neutrophilia and a left shift is typical, and leukopenia may be present in cases of sepsis.

Chapter: Reproductive, Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1398)

faqs.question: Can I wait until the morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because pyometra can cause a dog's condition to deteriorate rapidly or lead to a fatal uterine rupture, you should seek emergency care immediately regardless of the time of day.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of pyometra?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include increased thirst (polydipsia), frequent urination, lethargy, loss of appetite, vomiting, and a distended or painful abdomen.

faqs.question: Can a spayed dog get pyometra?
faqs.answer: It is extremely rare. However, if a small amount of uterine or ovarian tissue was left behind during surgery (stump pyometra), a similar infection can occur. Most cases involve intact female dogs.
